paragraphs.text: Two pieces of Feedback: First, what has been happening in the UK has me concerned. I don't only think that the "Online Safety Act" requiring platforms to ask for identification is plain and simply dumb, I think platforms in general shouldn't be allowed to ask for your ID in the EU. I am not familiar with current law, but if there are no or close to no rules on this, I would appreciate you addressing this. Second, there is a petition on "Stop Killing Games". I would like the Digital Fairness Act to include rules regarding software that is advertised as a product, but treated as a license. I think that a game is almost always a product, which is sold and after the purchase owned by the buyer. The only reasonable exception is when a game is only available through a subscription, granting the subscriber a license to play the game for the duration of the subscription. One consequence of this is that a developer should never be able to shut down a game (that is owned by the buyer), when it is reasonable to expect them to (at the very least) leave the offline component of the game in a functional state. What they also should be required to do is giving players a way to host the game themselves, be it through peer to peer connections or through giving the community the permission to host central servers (or other reasonable possibilities), unless they can provide exceptional reasons why they cannot do that. The Idea of "Stop Killing Games" can also be expanded to hardware that comes with software and "a license" to the software aswell as maintenance of that software. When support ends, there should be ways to keep the hardware functional, for example by making the software open source. This approach is a bit radical, but similar measures can also do the job, where a company cannot just abandon a product and force everyone to buy the new one. This behavior is not competitive in my opinion and can be observed at Microsoft, who announced to end support on pretty short notice (around 1.5 years in advance). I would like this issue to also be addressed in the Digital Fairness Act, because it is urgent and perfectly on topic
